items.mistake: Resolving conflicts by deleting markers and keeping whichever lines happen to be there.
items.fix: Read both sides, understand each intent, write the correct *combined* result, *then* remove markers.
items.why: Auto-removing markers picks an arbitrary side and silently loses one party's work.
items.example:
```
<<<<<<< HEAD
    timeout = 30   # YOUR change for low-latency endpoint
=======
    timeout = 60   # THEIR change for slow-LLM endpoint
>>>>>>> feature/slow-llm
# Wrong: just delete markers and ship whichever stuck.
# Right: refactor — pass timeout per endpoint, both intents preserved.
```

items.mistake: Forgetting to `git add` the resolved file before continuing.
items.fix: After editing, run `git add <file>` for every resolved file. `git status` will tell you if anything's still 'both modified'.
items.why: Git won't let you continue the merge/rebase if it thinks a file is still in conflict.
items.example:
```bash
git status   # must show all files green / staged, none 'both modified'
git rebase --continue
```

items.fix: Build, test, and inspect `git diff HEAD~1..HEAD` before pushing. Merge commits skip tests in many setups.
items.why: A bad resolution can compile but break runtime behaviour; CI on the merge commit catches what local tests skipped.
items.example:
```bash
pytest                 # run tests on the resolved tree
git diff HEAD~1..HEAD  # what's the merge actually adding?
```

items.mistake: Rebasing a long branch through 20 conflicts when merge would surface them once.
items.fix: Abort the rebase; merge `main` into the branch instead.
items.why: Rebase replays each commit individually — the same logical conflict can surface several times.
items.example:
```bash
git rebase --abort
git merge origin/main    # one conflict surface, one resolution
```

items.mistake: Committing notebook conflicts with raw JSON markers.
items.fix: Install `nbdime` for semantic notebook diffs, or `nbstripout` to strip outputs before commit.
items.why: `.ipynb` files mix code, outputs, metadata and execution counts — JSON-level conflict markers are unreadable.
